body.text: Nevermind childhood. My real origin story started when I joined the U.S. Air Force as an Officer in 2003.
It was the time period that forged my discipline, carved my leadership, and set a personal standard that has never left me — even if I wasn't always consciously aware of it.
In flight school, I was on a mission: to graduate at the top of my class so I could get my chosen airplane and duty location.
I graduated #1 of 57 from Joint Specialized Undergraduate Navigator Training (JSUNT) and took home both academic awards.
The General presenting the awards said to me, as I went to collect the second one from him, "You've got a bright career ahead, Lieutenant."
I loved the military life. The brotherhood you experience is really like no other. You partied together, learned together, and fought together. It redefined teamwork and camaraderie for me.
It gave me a place where I felt like I belonged.
I felt unstoppable. Upon my arrival at Davis-Monthan Air Force Base, I soaked up the sun, admired the mountains, and knew this was meant to be.

I was on "casual status" when I got introduced to the first squadron commander in our group. I started out as his Executive Officer  — and wasn't supposed to be assigned to his squadron but for a few weeks — but he loved me so much, he traded his next two assigned officers just to keep me.
That commander even blessed me with the role of Officer in Charge of Security — a privilege, he told me, rarely given to a new Lieutenant.
I killed it — like always. I embraced the Air Force core value of "Excellence in all we do."
My squadron commander loved me, my airmen loved me. I had the respect of those around me.
I trained. I deployed. I enabled combat missions that we wouldn't have otherwise been able to do because I was the only deployed "dual-qualified" Chief in my role.
Over 250 combat hours in hostile territory.
An Air Medal for meritorious service.
Then I came back home. And everything changed.
